evidence_quotes.quote: Distributors still have the ability to remotely disable or remove access to gameseven after theyve been sold.
if a company stops supporting a game, those who bought it should still be able to play it.

paragraphs.text: A clear gap in the current draft of the Digital Fairness Act is the lack of protection for consumers who lose access to digital games they have purchased. Today, most people buy their games digitally. We do so believing that paying for a game means well be able to access and play it whenever we want. Unfortunately, this isnt guaranteed. Distributors still have the ability to remotely disable or remove access to gameseven after theyve been sold. When this happens, consumers are left without compensation, alternatives, or any way to use what they paid for. This isnt just disappointingits fundamentally unfair. It undermines trust in digital markets and challenges the basic concept of ownership. This concern is not theoretical. The Stop Killing Games campaign has already gathered over 1.4 million signatures from people across the EU. That reflects a growing frustration and a clear call for better consumer protection. And what people are asking for is simple and fair: if a company stops supporting a game, those who bought it should still be able to play it. There are several reasonable ways to make this possible: enabling offline play, removing server dependencies, or offering a standalone version of the game that doesnt rely on a companys infrastructure. None of these options require companies to continue supporting or updating a game indefinitely. The only thing they require is the willingness to respect the customers right to access what theyve paid for. From a technical and business standpoint, this is absolutely possible. Many games already function without the need for constant updates or live servers. In many cases, a final patch or a simple change to unlock offline functionality would be enough. It wouldnt require ongoing customer service or new content. It would simply preserve access to what already belongs to the user. Without clear rules, the current system gives too much power to companies. They can sell a digital product, profit from it, and then later revoke access without facing any consequences. This would never be acceptable in the physical world. If someone buys a DVD, the studio cant show up years later and take it back. But in the digital space, this kind of behavior is increasingly commonand its eroding public trust. This situation discourages people from buying digital products and damages confidence in the long-term value of digital ownership. If the Digital Fairness Act wants to be true to its name, it must address this issue directly. Consumers should not be left empty-handed simply because a company decides to shut something down. To be clear, no one is asking for permanent support or never-ending updates. What people are asking for is the ability to keep using something they legally bought. Thats a reasonable expectation in any marketplacedigital or otherwise. By requiring that discontinued games remain accessible in a basic, playable form, the EU would be setting a balanced and responsible standard. Companies would still have the freedom to end sales or move on from older titles. But they would no longer be allowed to retroactively take those titles away from paying customers. Thats not just good consumer protection. Its common sense. Please value what the Stop Killing Games initiative proposes as part of this act!
